insights: Customer Support processes expenses 1.36x faster than Sales—the slowest department—despite handling the highest average expense amount ($85,059), indicating operational excellence is not driven by lower complexity or smaller ticket size.

Department incident count shows the strongest negative correlation with processing time (−0.777), meaning higher-volume departments consistently process faster—suggesting scale-driven learning effects or standardized workflows are key efficiency levers.

Miscellaneous expense proportion has a strong positive correlation with slower processing (0.548), and Customer Support has the lowest Miscellaneous share (5.3%), implying that ambiguous or unstructured expense categorization creates approval bottlenecks across departments.

Customer Support’s category mix—dominated by Assets (42.1%) and low Miscellaneous (5.3%)—correlates with faster processing, while Services and Miscellaneous proportions correlate positively with delays, revealing that category-specific workflow maturity drives departmental speed differences.

One-time expenses show a modest but consistent negative correlation with processing time (−0.241), and Customer Support has the second-highest One-time proportion (68.4%), suggesting streamlined, non-recurring workflows contribute to its speed advantage.

All departments except Customer Support have Recurring expense ratios above 43.8%, while Customer Support’s is only 31.6%—highlighting that recurring expense handling may be a systemic drag on efficiency due to manual renewal cycles or inconsistent approvals.
summary: The analysis confirms Customer Support achieves significantly faster expense processing (1386.4 hours avg) than other departments—not because of simpler or smaller expenses, but due to structural advantages: highest incident volume (19 cases), lowest Miscellaneous category usage (5.3%), strongest Assets focus (42.1%), and relatively high One-time expense share (68.4%). The dominant driver of cross-departmental efficiency is incident count (−0.777 correlation), indicating volume enables process standardization and learning effects; meanwhile, high Miscellaneous proportion (0.548 correlation) acts as a universal drag, reflecting poor upfront categorization and complex downstream approvals. Replicating Customer Support’s practices—especially its category discipline, volume-enabled workflow maturity, and One-time optimization—could yield up to 17.6% total processing time reduction. Next, priority should be placed on auditing and reclassifying Miscellaneous expenses, mapping Customer Support’s end-to-end workflow, and piloting workload consolidation to increase per-department incident volume.
